content:
# Acts Chapter 7 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 232-234 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_007_page_001.jpg, ACT_007_page_002.jpg, ACT_007_page_003.jpg

---

**1** And the high priest said, "Are these things so?" **2** And he said, "Brothers and fathers, hear me. The God of glory appeared to our father Abraham when he was in Mesopotamia, before he dwelt in Haran, **3** and said to him, 'Go out from your land and from your kindred, and come into the land which I will show you.' **4** Then he went out from the land of the Chaldeans and dwelt in Haran. And from there, after his father died, God removed him into this land in which you now dwell. **5** And he gave him no inheritance in it, not even a foot's length, but he promised to give it to him as a possession, and to his offspring after him, though he had no child. **6** And God spoke thus: that his offspring would be sojourners in a foreign land, and they would enslave them and mistreat them four hundred years. **7** 'And the nation which they serve I will judge,' said God, 'and after these things they shall come out and worship me in this place.'

**8** And he gave him the covenant of circumcision; and so Abraham begot Isaac and circumcised him on the eighth day, and Isaac begot Jacob, and Jacob the twelve patriarchs.

**9** And the patriarchs, being jealous, sold Joseph into Egypt; and God was with him **10** and rescued him out of all his afflictions, and gave him favor and wisdom before Pharaoh, king of Egypt, and he appointed him ruler over Egypt and over all his household.

**11** Now a famine came over all Egypt and Canaan, and great affliction, and our fathers found no food. **12** But when Jacob heard that there was grain in Egypt, he sent our fathers the first time. **13** And on the second visit, Joseph was made known to his brothers, and Joseph's family became known to Pharaoh. **14** And Joseph sent and summoned Jacob his father and all his kindred, seventy-five souls in all. **15** And Jacob went down into Egypt, and he died, he and our fathers, **16** and they were carried over to Shechem and laid in the tomb that Abraham had bought for a sum of silver from the sons of Hamor in Shechem.

**17** But as the time of the promise drew near, which God had promised to Abraham, the people grew and multiplied in Egypt, **18** until there arose another king over Egypt who did not know Joseph. **19** This one dealt craftily with our race and mistreated our fathers, forcing them to expose their infants so that they would not survive.

**20** At that time Moses was born, and he was beautiful before God; and he was nourished three months in his father's house. **21** And when he was exposed, the daughter of Pharaoh took him up and reared him as her own son. **22** And Moses was instructed in all the wisdom of the Egyptians, and he was mighty in his words and deeds.

**23** And when a period of forty years was fulfilled for him, it came into his heart to visit his brothers, the sons of Israel. **24** And seeing one of them being wronged, he defended him and avenged the one being oppressed, striking down the Egyptian. **25** And he supposed that his brothers would understand that God was giving them salvation through his hand, but they did not understand. **26** And on the following day he appeared to them as they were quarreling and tried to reconcile them to peace, saying, 'Men, you are brothers; why do you wrong one another?' **27** But the one who was wronging his neighbor thrust him aside, saying, 'Who appointed you a ruler and a judge over us? **28** Do you wish to kill me in the way you killed the Egyptian yesterday?' **29** And Moses fled at this word and became a sojourner in the land of Midian, where he begot two sons.

**30** And when forty years were fulfilled, an angel appeared to him in the wilderness of Mount Sinai, in a flame of fire in a bush. **31** And when Moses saw it, he marveled at the sight, and as he drew near to look, there came a voice of the Lord: **32** 'I am the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham and of Isaac and of Jacob.' And Moses trembled and did not dare to look. **33** And the Lord said to him, 'Remove the sandals from your feet, for the place on which you stand is holy ground. **34** I have surely seen the mistreatment of my people who are in Egypt, and I have heard their groaning, and I have come down to deliver them. And now come, I will send you to Egypt.'

**35** This Moses whom they rejected, saying, 'Who appointed you a ruler and a judge?' — this one God sent as both ruler and redeemer by the hand of the angel who appeared to him in the bush. **36** This one led them out, performing wonders and signs in the land of Egypt and in the Red Sea and in the wilderness for forty years. **37** This is the Moses who said to the sons of Israel, 'God will raise up for you a prophet like me from your brothers.' **38** This is the one who was in the assembly in the wilderness, with the angel who spoke to him at Mount Sinai, and with our fathers — the one who received living oracles to give to us, **39** to whom our fathers were unwilling to be obedient, but thrust him aside and turned in their hearts back to Egypt, **40** saying to Aaron, 'Make for us gods who will go before us; for this Moses who led us out from the land of Egypt — we do not know what has happened to him.' **41** And they made a calf in those days and brought a sacrifice to the idol and were rejoicing in the works of their hands.

**42** But God turned away and gave them over to worship the host of heaven, as it is written in the book of the prophets: 'Did you bring to me slain beasts and sacrifices during the forty years in the wilderness, O house of Israel? **43** And you took up the tent of Moloch and the star of the god Rephan, the images which you made to worship them; and I will remove you beyond Babylon.'

**44** The tent of the testimony was among our fathers in the wilderness, just as the one who spoke to Moses directed him to make it according to the pattern which he had seen. **45** And our fathers who received it in turn brought it in with Joshua when they dispossessed the nations whom God drove out before our fathers, until the days of David, **46** who found favor before God and asked to find a dwelling place for the God of Jacob. **47** But Solomon built him a house. **48** Yet the Most High does not dwell in things made by hands, as the prophet says:

**49** 'Heaven is my throne, and the earth is a footstool for my feet. What kind of house will you build for me, says the Lord, or what is the place of my rest? **50** Did not my hand make all these things?'

**51** You stiff-necked and uncircumcised in hearts and ears, you always resist the Holy Spirit; as your fathers did, so do you. **52** Which of the prophets did your fathers not persecute? And they killed those who announced beforehand the coming of the Righteous One, of whom you have now become betrayers and murderers — **53** you who received the law as delivered by angels and did not keep it.

**54** Now when they heard these things, they were cut to their hearts, and they gnashed their teeth at him. **55** But being full of the Holy Spirit, he gazed intently into heaven and saw the glory of God, and Jesus standing at the right hand of God, **56** and he said, "Behold, I see the heavens opened and the Son of Man standing at the right hand of God."

**57** But they cried out with a loud voice and stopped their ears, and they rushed upon him with one accord. **58** And casting him out of the city, they began to stone him. And the witnesses laid down their garments at the feet of a young man named Saul. **59** And they were stoning Stephen as he called out and said, "Lord Jesus, receive my spirit." **60** And falling on his knees, he cried out with a loud voice, "Lord, do not hold this sin against them." And having said this, he fell asleep.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"Brothers and fathers" (v. 2):** Greek *andres adelphoi kai pateres* — Stephen addresses the Sanhedrin with a double title of respect, acknowledging them both as fellow Israelites ("brothers") and as elders of authority ("fathers"). This respectful address is noteworthy given the severity of his subsequent indictment.

2. **"The God of glory" (v. 2):** Greek *ho theos tes doxes* (ὁ θεὸς τῆς δόξης). A rare title for God, found also in Psalm 29:3 (LXX 28:3). Stephen opens with this designation deliberately: the glory of God is not confined to the Temple, since it appeared to Abraham in Mesopotamia — a point central to his entire defense.

3. **"Seventy-five souls" (v. 14):** The Sinaiticus reads *psuchais hebdomekonta pente* (seventy-five), following the Septuagint reading of Genesis 46:27 and Exodus 1:5, which includes additional descendants of Joseph born in Egypt. The Hebrew Masoretic Text reads "seventy." Stephen, speaking Greek, naturally follows the LXX reckoning.

4. **"Beautiful before God" (v. 20):** Greek *asteios to theo* (ἀστεῖος τῷ θεῷ). The dative *to theo* functions as a dative of respect or intensification — "beautiful in the sight of God" or "exceedingly beautiful." The word *asteios* (from *astu*, "city") originally meant "refined, urbane" but came to denote physical beauty. The Septuagint uses this word of Moses in Exodus 2:2. Rendered formally to preserve the divine perspective on Moses' appearance.

5. **"Living oracles" (v. 38):** Greek *logia zonta* (λόγια ζῶντα). Stephen describes the law given at Sinai not as dead letters but as "living oracles" — words that carry divine life and ongoing authority. This is a crucial counterpoint to the accusation that Stephen speaks against Moses and the law; he is affirming their vital, God-given character even as he charges his hearers with disobedience to them.

6. **"Tent of Moloch... star of the god Rephan" (v. 43):** Stephen quotes Amos 5:25-27 following the Septuagint, which reads "Rephan" (Ῥαιφάν) where the Hebrew has "Kiyyun" (likely a reference to the Assyrian deity Saturn/Kaiwan). The LXX also reads "Babylon" where the Hebrew text of Amos has "Damascus" — a prophetic re-reading in light of the later Babylonian exile. The Sinaiticus preserves the LXX form.

7. **"Tent of the testimony" (v. 44):** Greek *he skene tou martyriou* (ἡ σκηνὴ τοῦ μαρτυρίου). Stephen contrasts the portable, divinely-patterned tent (*skene*) with the fixed Temple (*oikos*, v. 47) built by Solomon. His argument builds toward the point that the tabernacle, made according to the heavenly pattern shown to Moses, represents the proper mode of God's dwelling among his people — mobile and not confined.

8. **"The Most High does not dwell in things made by hands" (v. 48):** Greek *ouch ho hupsistos en cheiropoietois katoikei*. The term *cheiropoietos* ("made by hands") carries negative connotations in the LXX, where it frequently describes idols. Stephen does not condemn the Temple outright but insists that God transcends it, citing Isaiah 66:1-2. The Sinaiticus text is clear here.

9. **"Uncircumcised in hearts and ears" (v. 51):** Greek *aperitmetoi kardiais kai tois osin*. Stephen inverts the charge against him: it is not he who opposes Moses, but his accusers who, despite their physical circumcision, remain uncircumcised in the inner faculties of reception — heart and ears. The language echoes Deuteronomy 10:16, Jeremiah 4:4, and Jeremiah 6:10.

10. **"The Righteous One" (v. 52):** Greek *tou dikaiou* (τοῦ δικαίου). A recognized messianic title (cf. Isaiah 53:11; Acts 3:14, 22:14). Stephen brings his historical survey to its climax: the pattern of rejecting God's appointed deliverers — Joseph, Moses — culminates in the murder of the Righteous One himself.

11. **"He fell asleep" (v. 60):** Greek *ekoimēthē* (ἐκοιμήθη) — the same verb used of natural sleep, employed here as a euphemism for death. This is the earliest Christian use of "falling asleep" for the death of a believer in narrative literature. The peaceful language stands in stark contrast to the violence of the stoning, and deliberately echoes Jesus' words from the cross: Stephen's prayer "Lord, do not hold this sin against them" parallels Luke 23:34.

— LOGOS
